lessons.title: Fun with Rhyming Words
lessons.subject: English

keyVocabulary.definition: words that sound the same at the end
what we hear when someone talks
the last part of a word
things that repeat in the same way
to hear carefully with your ears

lessons.warmUp: Let's clap and say! I'll say a word, you clap and repeat it! Cat! *clap clap* Hat! *clap clap* Do they sound the same at the end? Yes! They rhyme!
lessons.mainExplanation: Rhyming words sound the same at the end! Cat and hat both end with 'at' sound. Dog and frog both end with 'og' sound. When words rhyme, they make a special sound pattern that's fun to hear and say!

workedExamples.label: Example 1
workedExamples.question: Do 'sun' and 'fun' rhyme?
workedExamples.answer: Yes! Both words end with 'un' sound. Sun, fun - they rhyme!

workedExamples.label: Example 2
workedExamples.question: Do 'ball' and 'tall' rhyme?
workedExamples.answer: Yes! Both words end with 'all' sound. Ball, tall - they rhyme!

workedExamples.label: Example 3
workedExamples.question: Do 'bear' and 'car' rhyme?
workedExamples.answer: No! Bear ends with 'ear' and car ends with 'ar'. They don't rhyme!

easy.question: Cat and ___ rhyme. What word sounds like cat?
easy.hint: Think of an animal that squeaks

easy.question: Match the rhyming words: sun, fun, run, gun
easy.hint: Words that sound the same at the end

easy.question: Dog and ___ rhyme. What sounds like dog?

medium.question: Ball and ___ rhyme. Fill in the rhyming word.
Match these rhyming pairs: bee, tree, see, me
medium.hint: Listen for the same ending sound

challenge.question: Make a rhyme! House and ___ sound the same at the end.
challenge.hint: Think of a tiny animal that squeaks

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with CAT?
quiz.explanation: CAT and HAT both end with the same sound -AT

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with SUN?
quiz.explanation: SUN and FUN both end with the same sound -UN

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with BIG?
quiz.explanation: BIG and PIG both end with the same sound -IG

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with BLUE?
quiz.explanation: BLUE and TWO both end with the same sound -OO

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with BEAR?
quiz.explanation: BEAR and CHAIR both end with the same sound -AIR

lessons.answerKey: 1. A) HAT (rhymes with CAT), 2. B) FUN (rhymes with SUN), 3. A) PIG (rhymes with BIG), 4. B) TWO (rhymes with BLUE), 5. B) CHAIR (rhymes with BEAR)
lessons.teacherNotes: Keep energy high with movement and gestures when saying rhyming words. Many PreK children confuse rhyming with words that start the same (cat/car) - emphasize listening to word endings. Use exaggerated pronunciation of rhyming endings to help children hear the pattern.
lessons.additionalResources: Picture cards with rhyming pairs, nursery rhyme books, audio recordings of simple poems, movement scarves for rhyming dances
lessons.description: Children will discover rhyming words through fun poems and songs. They will learn to hear similar sounds and make their own rhymes.

resourceSections.teacherGuide:
**Fun with Rhyming Words - 30 minute lesson**

**Circle Time Introduction (5 minutes)**
- Gather children in circle on carpet
- Sing 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star' together
- Ask: 'Did you notice words that sounded the same? Star and are sound alike!'
- Explain: 'Today we're learning about rhyming - words that sound the same at the end'

**Exploring Rhyming Sounds (8 minutes)**
- Show picture cards: cat, hat, bat, mat
- Say each word clearly, emphasizing the '-at' ending
- Have children repeat: 'Cat, hat - they rhyme!'
- Introduce second family: dog, frog, log, hog
- Children clap when they hear rhyming words
- Practice with sun/fun, book/look, red/bed

**Interactive Rhyming Game (10 minutes)**
- 'Rhyme Time Dance': Play music, hold up picture cards
- Children dance when words rhyme, freeze when they don't
- Start with obvious pairs: tree/bee, car/star
- Include non-rhyming pairs: apple/dog, fish/moon
- Let children suggest rhyming words for simple prompts

**Simple Poem Listening (5 minutes)**
- Read 'Hickory Dickory Dock' with dramatic voices
- Point out rhyming words: dock/clock
- Read 'Humpty Dumpty', emphasizing wall/fall
- Children join in on familiar rhyming endings

**Creating Rhymes Together (2 minutes)**
- Start silly sentences: 'I see a cat wearing a ___' (hat)
- 'The big dog sat on a ___' (log/frog)
- Encourage creative, silly answers
- Celebrate all attempts, even if not perfect rhymes
resourceSections.teacherNotes: Keep energy high with movement and gestures when saying rhyming words. Many PreK children confuse rhyming with words that start the same (cat/car) - emphasize listening to word endings. Use exaggerated pronunciation of rhyming endings to help children hear the pattern. Some children may suggest words that don't rhyme - praise their participation and gently model the correct rhyme. Visual picture cards are essential as many children are still developing phonemic awareness.

textbookPages.title: Welcome to Rhyme Time!
textbookPages.body:
Hi there, little readers! Today we're going on a special adventure with words. We're going to learn about rhyming words - words that sound the same at the end! When you hear words like 'cat' and 'hat', do you notice how they sound alike? That's because they rhyme!

Rhyming words are like best friends because they share the same ending sound. Just like how best friends like to play together, rhyming words like to be together in poems and songs. Listen carefully with your ears - you'll start to hear rhymes everywhere!
bullets: Rhyming words sound the same at the end
Listen carefully to hear the patterns
Rhymes make language fun and musical
activities: Clap your hands when you hear rhyming words!
textbookPages.callout: Cat and Hat are rhyming friends!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A smiling cat wearing a colorful hat

textbookPages.title: What Makes Words Rhyme?
textbookPages.body:
Let's explore what makes words special rhyming partners! When we say 'dog' and 'frog', both words end with the same 'og' sound. That's what makes them rhyme!

Try saying these words out loud: sun, fun, run. Do you hear how they all end the same way? The 'un' sound at the end is what makes them a rhyming family. Now try ball, tall, wall - they're another rhyming family because they all end with 'all'!

Sometimes words might start differently but if they end the same, they still rhyme. Big and pig start with different sounds, but they both end with 'ig' - so they rhyme perfectly!
bullets: Words rhyme when they have the same ending sound
Different beginnings are okay - listen to the endings
Practice saying rhyming words out loud
activities: Say 'dog' and 'frog' - can you hear the rhyme?
textbookPages.callout: The ending sound is the most important part!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A green frog sitting next to a happy dog

textbookPages.title: Meet the Rhyming Families
textbookPages.body:
Just like you have a family, words have families too! Let's meet some word families that love to rhyme together.

The 'AT' family includes: cat, hat, bat, mat, and rat. They all love to play together because they sound alike! The 'UN' family has: sun, fun, run, and bun. These words are always having fun in the sun!

There's also the 'ALL' family with: ball, tall, wall, and fall. And don't forget the 'EE' family: bee, tree, see, and me. Each family has its own special sound that makes all the words in it rhyme together.
bullets: Word families share the same ending sound
AT family: cat, hat, bat, mat
UN family: sun, fun, run
ALL family: ball, tall, wall
activities: Can you think of another word for the AT family?
textbookPages.callout: Word families make learning rhymes easy!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Different animals representing each word family playing together

textbookPages.title: Let's Practice Rhyming!
textbookPages.body:
Now it's time to practice finding rhymes! Look at the word 'bee'. What other words sound like bee at the end? Tree! See! Me! You're getting good at this!

Try this fun game: I'll give you a word, and you think of a word that rhymes with it. Ready? The word is 'car'. What rhymes with car? Star! Far! You're doing great!

Remember, when you're looking for rhymes, listen carefully to the ending sound. Sometimes two words might look different when you see them written, but if they sound the same at the end when you say them out loud, they rhyme!
bullets: Listen to ending sounds carefully
Practice with your favorite words
It's okay if words look different but sound the same
activities: Find a word that rhymes with 'house'
Clap when you say rhyming pairs
textbookPages.callout: Practice makes perfect at finding rhymes!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Children sitting in a circle, clapping and smiling while practicing rhymes

textbookPages.title: Rhymes in Poems and Songs
textbookPages.body:
Rhyming words make poems and songs extra special and fun to remember! Let's look at a simple poem: 'The little cat sat on a mat, wearing a big blue hat!' Can you hear the rhymes? Cat, mat, and hat all rhyme together!

Nursery rhymes are full of rhyming words too. In 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star', the words 'star' and 'are' rhyme. In 'Humpty Dumpty', we hear 'wall' and 'fall' rhyming together.

When we sing songs with rhyming words, they're easier to remember and more fun to sing. The rhymes help our brains remember what comes next in the song or poem!
bullets: Poems use lots of rhyming words
Nursery rhymes are full of rhymes
Rhymes help us remember songs and poems
activities: Listen for rhymes in 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star'
textbookPages.callout: Rhymes make poems and songs memorable!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Children singing together with musical notes floating around them

textbookPages.title: Create Your Own Rhyming Fun!
textbookPages.body:
Now that you know about rhymes, let's make some of our own! Start with a simple word like 'red'. What rhymes with red? Bed! So you could make a silly sentence like 'The red teddy bear sleeps in my bed!'

Try making rhymes with your name or your favorite things. If your name is Jake, you could say 'Jake likes to bake a cake!' If you love dogs, you might say 'My dog likes to sit on a log!'

Don't worry if your rhymes are silly - the sillier, the better! Rhyming is supposed to be fun. You can make up stories, songs, or just silly sentences with your rhyming words.
bullets: Start with simple words you know
Make silly sentences with rhymes
Use words about things you love
Silly rhymes are the most fun!
activities: Make a rhyme using your favorite color
Create a silly sentence with 'pig' and 'big'
textbookPages.callout: Your rhymes can be as silly as you want!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A child laughing while writing in a colorful notebook with crayons scattered around

textbookPages.title: Become a Rhyme Detective!
textbookPages.body:
Congratulations! You're now a Rhyme Detective! Your job is to listen for rhyming words everywhere you go. You might hear them in songs on the radio, in books that grown-ups read to you, or even in conversations!

When you're listening to stories, keep your detective ears open for rhyming words. When you hear two words that sound the same at the end, you've found a rhyme! You can point them out to your family and friends.

Remember, not all words that sound similar are perfect rhymes, and that's okay! Sometimes words are close enough to be fun to say together. The most important thing is that you're listening and having fun with language!
bullets: Listen for rhymes in everyday life
Point out rhymes to family and friends
Keep your detective ears open
Have fun discovering new rhymes
activities: Find rhymes in your favorite bedtime story
Listen for rhymes during car rides
textbookPages.figureCaption: A child wearing a detective hat and holding a magnifying glass, looking excited about finding rhymes

textbookPages.title: You're a Rhyming Star!
textbookPages.body:
Amazing work, rhyming star! You've learned so much about how words can sound alike and play together. You can now hear when words rhyme, and you can even make up your own rhyming words and sentences!

Keep practicing with your family and friends. Read lots of books with rhymes, sing songs, and don't forget to make up your own silly rhyming games. Every time you notice rhyming words, you're becoming an even better listener and speaker.

Remember, rhyming is all around us - in books, songs, poems, and even in the way we talk every day. Now that you know the secret of rhyming words, you'll hear them everywhere! Keep having fun with words, and keep making beautiful rhymes!
bullets: You're now a rhyming expert!
Keep practicing with books and songs
Share your rhyming skills with others
Have fun making up new rhymes every day
activities: Teach someone else about rhyming words
Make up a rhyming song about your day
textbookPages.figureCaption: Happy children celebrating with confetti and stars, holding books and musical instruments

lessons.title: Fun with Simple Poems
lessons.subject: English
learningObjectives: Identify rhyming words in simple poems
Listen to and repeat short poems
Recognize that poems can be about animals and toys

keyVocabulary.definition: special words that sound nice together
words that sound the same at the end
use your ears to hear sounds
living things like cats, dogs, and birds

lessons.warmUp: Let's clap and say animal sounds! When I say 'cat,' you say 'meow' and clap twice. Ready? Cat! Dog! Cow! Duck! Now let's find words that sound the same at the end!
lessons.mainExplanation: Poems are fun word songs! Some words rhyme - they sound the same at the end, like 'cat' and 'hat.' We can make poems about animals and toys we love. Listen carefully and clap along!
workedExamples:
POEM 1 — My Cat:
I have a little cat
Who likes to wear a hat
She sits upon my mat
And purrs just like that

POEM 2 — Happy Dog:
My dog likes to run
Out in the bright sun
We play and have fun
Until the day is done

POEM 3 — Teddy Bear:
Teddy bear so soft and brown
Never wears a little frown
Sleeps with me when I lie down
Best friend in our little town

easy.question: What rhymes with 'cat'? Choose: bat, dog, car
easy.hint: Listen for the ending sound: cat, ___at

easy.question: Match the rhyming words: sun → ___. Choose: fun, big, red
easy.hint: Both words end with the same sound

easy.question: Fill in the blank: The dog likes to play, the dog likes to ___. (say, run, eat)

medium.question: Complete the poem: 'I see a bee, flying by ___.' (me, you, there)
Match to finish the rhyme: 'My toy car goes far, my toy ___ goes ___.' Match: truck/luck, ball/tall
medium.hint: Find words that sound the same at the end

challenge.question: Fill in TWO rhyming words: 'The ___ is blue, the ___ is too.' (sky/pie, cat/dog, big/small)
challenge.hint: Both words should end with the same sound

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with cat?
quiz.explanation: Hat and cat both end with the same sound -at

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with tree?
quiz.explanation: Bee and tree both end with the same sound -ee

quiz.question: What do we call words that sound the same at the end?
quiz.explanation: Words that sound the same at the end are called rhyme words

quiz.question: Which word rhymes with toy?
quiz.explanation: Boy and toy both end with the same sound -oy

quiz.question: What should we do when we hear a poem?
quiz.explanation: We should listen carefully when we hear a poem

lessons.answerKey:
1. A) hat (rhymes with cat)
2. B) bee (rhymes with tree)
3. C) rhyme words (words that sound the same)
4. B) boy (rhymes with toy)
5. C) listen (what we do with poems)
lessons.teacherNotes: Read each poem twice, having children clap on the rhyming words. Point out how 'cat/hat/mat' and 'run/sun/fun' sound alike at the end. Encourage children to repeat the poems with you using actions. Use picture cards and props to make poems more engaging.
lessons.additionalResources: Picture cards for rhyming words, toy props, animal pictures, rhythm sticks for clapping, simple poem books
lessons.description: Children will listen to fun animal poems and learn about rhyming words. They will clap along and make their own simple rhymes.

resourceSections.teacherGuide:
**Opening Circle Time (5 min):** Gather children on carpet in circle. Say 'Today we're going to play with poems - poems are like word songs!' Clap hands rhythmically and have children copy. Introduce that poems have special words that sound alike at the end.

**Rhyming Word Introduction (8 min):** Show picture cards of cat and hat. Say both words slowly, emphasizing the '-at' sound. Ask children to repeat. Explain 'These words rhyme - they sound the same at the end!' Show more pairs: dog/frog, bee/tree, toy/boy. Have children clap when they hear rhyming words.

**Animal Poem Activity (10 min):** Recite simple animal poem: 'I see a cat wearing a hat, sitting on a mat, imagine that!' Use props or pictures. Have children repeat line by line. Ask 'What words sound the same?' Point to rhyming words. Repeat with second poem about a dog and frog.

**Toy Poem Activity (8 min):** Share toy poem: 'My favorite toy brings me joy, I play with it, oh boy oh boy!' Show actual toys as you recite. Have children echo each line. Ask them to identify rhyming words (toy/joy/boy). Let children hold toys mentioned in poem.

**Interactive Movement (7 min):** Stand up for action poem: 'Jump like a bunny, hop hop hop! Run like a puppy, don't you stop! Fly like a birdie in the sky, wave your wings up high!' Have children act out movements. Emphasize rhyming pairs through movement.

**Closing Circle (7 min):** Sit back in circle. Ask 'What makes words rhyme?' Review that rhyming words sound the same at the end. Have children share their favorite rhyming pair from today. End with simple chant: 'Poems are fun, poems are neat, clap your hands to the beat!' Children clap along.
resourceSections.teacherNotes: Keep picture cards and props nearby for visual support - PreK students learn better with concrete objects. Many children this age may not yet hear subtle sound differences, so exaggerate rhyming endings and use familiar word pairs. If children struggle with rhyming concept, focus on exact matches first (cat/bat/hat) before introducing near-rhymes. Some students may want to share non-rhyming words - praise their participation and gently redirect with 'That's a great word! Let's find one that sounds like cat at the end.' Use hand gestures and clapping to help children feel the rhythm of poems, as this supports their phonological awareness development.

textbookPages.title: Welcome to Poetry Land!
textbookPages.body:
Hello, little poets! Today we're going on a special adventure to Poetry Land, where words dance and play together. In Poetry Land, some words are best friends because they sound alike at the end. These special friend-words are called rhyming words!

Look around you - do you see a cat? What if that cat wore a hat? Cat and hat are rhyming friends because they both end with the same sound: -at! When we put rhyming words together in a special way, we make something magical called a poem.

Poems are like word-songs that make us smile. They can tell stories about our favorite animals, toys, and all the things we love. Are you ready to discover the magic of rhyming words? Let's clap our hands and begin our poetry adventure!
bullets: Poems are special word-songs
Rhyming words sound the same at the end
We can make poems about animals and toys
activities: Clap your hands and say 'cat, hat, mat' - do you hear how they sound alike?
textbookPages.callout: Rhyming words are like best friends - they sound the same at the end!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A happy cat wearing a colorful hat, sitting on a soft mat

textbookPages.title: My Little Cat
textbookPages.body:
Let's meet our first poetry friend - a sweet little cat! This cat is very special because she loves to dress up and play. Listen to her poem and see if you can find the rhyming words.

I have a little cat
Who likes to wear a hat
She sits upon my mat
And purrs just like that!

Did you hear the rhyming words? Cat, hat, mat, and that all sound alike at the end! They all have the '-at' sound. When you say them together, they make a happy rhythm. Try clapping along as you say the poem - clap-clap on each rhyming word!

This little cat is so happy because she found her rhyming friends. Just like the cat found words that match her name, we can find rhyming friends for other words too. What other words can you think of that rhyme with cat?
bullets: Cat, hat, mat, that all rhyme
Rhyming words make a happy rhythm
We can clap along with poems
activities: Say the cat poem and clap on the rhyming words
Think of more words that rhyme with cat
textbookPages.callout: All the '-at' words are rhyming friends!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A tabby cat wearing a red hat, sitting peacefully on a blue mat

textbookPages.title: Happy Puppy Play
textbookPages.body:
Now let's meet a bouncy, happy puppy who loves to play outside! This puppy has so much energy and loves sunny days. Listen to his special poem and find the rhyming words.

My dog likes to run
Out in the bright sun
We play and have fun
Until the day is done!

What rhyming words did you hear? Run, sun, fun, and done all sound the same at the end - they all have the '-un' sound! This puppy found four rhyming friends to play with in his poem.

When we say this poem, we can pretend to run in place like the happy puppy. We can also stretch our arms up high like the bright sun in the sky. Poems are even more fun when we move our bodies along with the words! The puppy runs and plays until the day is done, just like how we learn and play until it's time to rest.
bullets: Run, sun, fun, done all rhyme with '-un'
We can move our bodies while saying poems
The puppy plays from morning until night
activities: Act out running in place while saying the puppy poem
Stretch your arms up high like the sun
textbookPages.callout: Moving with poems makes them even more fun!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A golden retriever puppy running happily in a sunny park

textbookPages.title: My Teddy Bear
textbookPages.body:
Every child needs a special friend, and teddy bears make the very best friends of all! This teddy bear is soft, cuddly, and never gets grumpy. Let's read his cozy poem together.

Teddy bear so soft and brown
Never wears a little frown
Sleeps with me when I lie down
Best friend in our little town!

Did you find the rhyming words? Brown, frown, down, and town all end with the '-own' sound. These words create a gentle, sleepy rhythm - perfect for a bedtime poem about a cuddly teddy bear.

Teddy bears are wonderful because they're always there when we need a hug. They sleep with us at night and keep us company during the day. When we say this poem, we can hug our own teddy bear (or pretend to hug one). The rhyming words make the poem sound like a gentle lullaby that helps us feel safe and loved.
bullets: Brown, frown, down, town all rhyme with '-own'
Teddy bears are special friends who never leave us
This poem sounds like a gentle lullaby
activities: Hug a teddy bear while saying the poem
Say the poem in a soft, gentle voice
textbookPages.callout: Teddy bears are the best friends because they never frown!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A soft brown teddy bear with a happy smile, perfect for cuddling

textbookPages.title: Find the Rhyming Friends
textbookPages.body:
Now it's your turn to be a rhyme detective! We've learned about words that sound the same at the end, and now we can practice finding rhyming friends everywhere we look.

Remember our rhyming families? The '-at' family has cat, hat, mat, and bat. The '-un' family has run, sun, fun, and gun. The '-own' family has brown, frown, down, and town. Each family has words that sound alike at the end.

Let's play a rhyming game! When I say a word, you try to think of another word that rhymes with it. Ready? What rhymes with 'bee'? If you said 'tree' or 'me' or 'see,' you're exactly right! What about 'toy'? Yes - 'boy' and 'joy' are perfect rhyming friends for toy.

The more we practice finding rhyming words, the better we get at making our own poems. Soon you'll be able to create poems about your favorite animals, toys, and anything else you love!
bullets: Rhyming words belong to the same sound family
We can be rhyme detectives and find rhyming friends
Practice helps us get better at making poems
activities: Think of words that rhyme with bee
Find rhyming friends for the word toy
textbookPages.callout: Every word has rhyming friends waiting to be found!
textbookPages.figureCaption: Children sitting in a circle, raising their hands excitedly to share rhyming words

textbookPages.title: Creating Our Own Poems
textbookPages.body:
You've learned so much about rhyming words and poems! Now comes the most exciting part - making your very own poem. Don't worry if it feels tricky at first. Even the best poets started by learning about rhyming words, just like you did today.

Think about your favorite animal or toy. Let's say you choose a bird. What words rhyme with bird? You might think of 'heard' or 'word.' Now you can start building a simple poem: 'I saw a little bird, the sweetest I have heard!'

Or maybe you want to write about your favorite ball. What rhymes with ball? How about 'tall' or 'call' or 'wall'? You could make a poem like: 'I have a bouncy ball, it bounces very tall!'

Remember, poems don't have to be perfect. They just need to be fun and come from your heart. The most important thing is that you enjoy playing with words and finding rhyming friends. Every great poet started exactly where you are right now!
bullets: Start with your favorite animal or toy
Find words that rhyme with your chosen word
Poems should be fun and come from your heart
activities: Choose your favorite animal and find rhyming words
Try making a two-line poem about something you love
textbookPages.callout: Every great poet started as a beginner, just like you!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A child sitting at a small table, drawing pictures and writing their first poem with a big smile

textbookPages.title: Sharing Our Poems
textbookPages.body:
Congratulations, young poets! You've discovered the wonderful world of rhyming words and poems. You've learned that poems are special word-songs that make us happy, and that rhyming words are like best friends who sound alike at the end.

Poetry is all around us - in the songs we sing, the games we play, and the stories we tell. Every time you notice words that sound alike, you're thinking like a poet. Every time you clap along to a rhythm, you're celebrating the music in words.

Poems can be about anything that makes you happy: your family, your pets, your favorite foods, or even your dreams. The most wonderful thing about poetry is that there's no wrong way to do it. Your poems are special because they come from you, and nobody else in the whole world can write poems exactly the way you do.

Keep playing with words, keep finding rhyming friends, and keep making poems. Poetry Land is always open, and there are always new adventures waiting for curious young poets like you!
bullets: Poetry is everywhere around us
Your poems are special because they come from you
There's no wrong way to write a poem
activities: Share your favorite rhyming words with someone you love
Look for rhyming words in songs and stories
textbookPages.callout: You are a poet, and your words are special!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A group of happy children sharing their poems, with colorful drawings and words floating around them like magic

textbookPages.title: Poetry Adventures Continue
textbookPages.body:
Your journey in Poetry Land has just begun! Now that you know about rhyming words and how to make simple poems, you can find poetry everywhere you go. Listen to the words in your favorite songs - do you hear rhyming friends? Look at the books you read - many stories have rhyming words too.

At home, you can play rhyming games with your family. Take turns saying a word and finding rhyming friends for it. You can make up silly poems about what you had for breakfast, what you see outside your window, or what you want to do tomorrow.

Remember the poems we learned today: the cat with the hat, the dog in the sun, and the brown teddy bear. These poems are now part of your poetry treasure chest. You can say them whenever you want to feel happy or help someone else smile.

As you grow bigger, you'll learn even more about poetry. But the most important thing you learned today will always stay with you: words are powerful, poems are fun, and you have the magic inside you to create beautiful things with language. Keep exploring, keep creating, and keep celebrating the joy of words!
bullets: Poetry can be found in songs, books, and everyday life
You can play rhyming games with family and friends
You have the magic inside you to create with words
activities: Listen for rhyming words in your favorite songs
Make up a silly poem about your breakfast
textbookPages.callout: The magic of poetry lives inside you forever!
textbookPages.figureCaption: A child walking on a path made of colorful words and letters, heading toward a bright, welcoming future
